y="45" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="400" fill="#7c8aa0" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" >count</text></svg>
blocks.cap: The mode is the value that shows up most often, so it stands as the tallest bar. Here that is the value \(4\), which appears \(4\) times.

blocks.x: The mode. The mode is the value that appears most often. The mean and median care about size and position, but the mode only cares about frequency, which is what makes it the natural "typical" for choices and categories. A shoe store stocks the most common size, not the average one, and a survey reports the most frequent answer. Frequency is the whole story, so a list where every value is different has no mode at all, and a list where two values tie for most frequent has two modes.

A list can have more than one mode. A survey records these numbers: \(2, 5, 2, 7, 5, 9, 5, 2\). Both \(2\) and \(5\) appear three times, which is more often than any other value. A value counts as a mode when it ties for the most frequent. How many values are modes?
hints: Tally how many times each different value shows up. Count the \(2\)s, the \(5\)s, the \(7\), and the \(9\) separately.
The mode is the value with the highest count, but if two values share that same top count, they are both modes. Look for which values reach the largest tally and count how many there are.
blocks.sol: Count how often each value appears. The value \(2\) shows up three times, the value \(5\) shows up three times, the value \(7\) once, and the value \(9\) once. The most frequent count is three, and two different values reach it, so both \(2\) and \(5\) are modes. That is what "multimodal" means, a list with more than one mode. Counting the values that tie for most frequent gives $$\boxed{2}.$$ There are two modes.

blocks.x: A gym sets your membership tier by the median of your nine monthly visit counts. Seven months in, your counts are \(20, 8, 16, 5, 22, 12, 16\). You have two months left, and you can visit at most \(30\) times in a month. What is the highest median you can reach across all nine months? Give the number.
hints: With nine values, the median is the \(5\)th number once they are sorted, so the whole question is where that middle slot lands.

Each new month can be at most \(30\), which is larger than everything you already have, so both new values drop in above your current numbers and none of them can slide into the \(5\)th slot from below.
blocks.sol: The median of nine values is the \(5\)th value once they are sorted, so the goal is to make that \(5\)th slot as large as possible. Sort the seven known counts, giving $$5,\ 8,\ 12,\ 16,\ 16,\ 20,\ 22.$$ Now add the two remaining months. Each can be at most \(30\), and \(30\) is bigger than every number already on the list, so both new values land at the top no matter what you pick. Adding two numbers only at the high end shifts nothing below the middle, so the \(5\)th of the nine sorted values stays put at the \(5\)th of the original seven, which is \(16\). Pushing the new months higher just makes the top of the list bigger, never the middle, so the best you can do is $$\boxed{16}.$$ The highest median you can reach is \(16\).

blocks.x: A gymnast's five routine scores average \(9.2\). She has one routine left, and she wants her average across all six scores to stay exactly \(9.2\). What score must she earn on the sixth routine? Give your answer as a decimal.
hints: The mean is the total of all scores divided by how many there are. With five scores averaging \(9.2\), the current total is \(5 \times 9.2 = 46\).

After the sixth routine you will divide a new total by \(6\), and you want that to equal \(9.2\). Ask what sixth score keeps the total-over-count balanced, and notice that adding one more copy of the mean itself leaves the mean untouched.
blocks.sol:
Use the fact that the mean is the total divided by the count. The five scores average \(9.2\), so their total is \(5 \times 9.2 = 46\). Call the sixth score \(x\). For the six-score average to stay \(9.2\), the new total \(46 + x\) divided by \(6\) must equal \(9.2\), giving
$$\frac{46 + x}{6} = 9.2.$$
Multiply both sides by \(6\), so \(46 + x = 55.2\), and subtract \(46\) to get \(x = 9.2\). This matches the shortcut worth remembering. Adding one more value that equals the current mean leaves the mean exactly where it was, since you are adding one more average-sized share to an average-sized pile. She must score $$\boxed{9.2}.$$

blocks.x: The balancing point. The mean is the balance point of the data. Measure how far each value sits above or below the mean, and those gaps cancel out exactly, so they always sum to zero. That one fact explains a lot. Adding a new value equal to the mean changes nothing, since it adds no gap to either side. A value above the mean tips the balance up, and one below tips it down, $$\text{sum of gaps above} = \text{sum of gaps below}.$$ So a single far-off outlier, with a huge gap on one side, can drag the mean a surprising distance to keep the two sides even.

A staircase has an LED on every step. The steps show \(7, 11, 15, 19,\) and so on, going up by \(4\) each step, with the top step showing \(43\). What is the mean of all the numbers shown? Give the number.
hints: The numbers are evenly spaced, each one \(4\) more than the last, so pair them from the outside in. The smallest \(7\) pairs with the largest \(43\), the next \(11\) pairs with \(39\), and so on, just like pairing the ends when you add \(1\) up to \(n\).

Every pair adds to the same total, \(7+43 = 50\), so every number sits the same distance above or below the center. That means you never have to add the whole list, since the mean is just the value halfway between the two ends.
blocks.sol:
The numbers are evenly spaced, each one \(4\) above the one before, so a shortcut works. Pair them from the ends inward, giving \(7\) with \(43\), then \(11\) with \(39\), then \(15\) with \(35\), and each pair sums to \(50\). Because the pairs are all equal, the values are perfectly balanced around one center, so the mean equals the median equals the average of the first and last term. That average is
$$\frac{7 + 43}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = \boxed{25}.$$
No full sum needed. For any evenly spaced list, the mean is just the average of the two ends, which here is \(25\).

blocks.x: Evenly spaced lists. When the values step up by a constant amount, the list is symmetric about its center. Each value below the middle is matched by one an equal distance above it, so those pairs balance out and the mean lands exactly at the median. Both equal the average of the first and last value, $$\text{mean}=\frac{\text{first}+\text{last}}{2}.$$ You get the center from just the two ends, with no need to add everything up. This is why a run of consecutive whole numbers, or any list with a fixed gap, gives up its mean at a glance.
A set of five positive whole numbers has a mean of \(16\), a median of \(15\), and \(15\) is its only mode. What is the greatest value the largest number in the set could be? Give the number.
hints: A mean of \(16\) across five numbers fixes the total. Multiply to get the sum, \(5 \times 16 = 80\), so however you fill the five slots they must add to \(80\).

Sort the five numbers. The median is the 3rd value, so the middle slot is \(15\). For \(15\) to be the only mode it has to appear at least twice, so make the 4th slot \(15\) too, and to leave the largest as big as possible make the two smallest slots the tiniest distinct positive integers that do not tie \(15\), namely \(1\) and \(2\).
blocks.sol:
The mean tells you the sum. Five numbers averaging \(16\) add up to \(5 \times 16 = 80\). Sort the set from smallest to largest and fill the five slots to push the top number as high as possible. The median is the 3rd value, so slot 3 is \(15\). For \(15\) to be the only mode it must appear at least twice and more often than anything else, so put another \(15\) in slot 4. To make the largest number big, shrink the first two slots as much as the rules allow. They must be positive whole numbers, and they cannot both be \(15\) or a repeated value that ties \(15\) for mode, so the smallest safe choice is \(1\) and \(2\). That leaves the largest number as the rest of the total,
$$80 - (1 + 2 + 15 + 15) = \boxed{47}.$$
Checking, the set \(\{1, 2, 15, 15, 47\}\) has sum \(80\) so mean \(16\), middle value \(15\) so median \(15\), and \(15\) is the only repeat so the only mode. The greatest the largest number can be is \(47\).

blocks.label: Fun fact
blocks.x: The word "average" traces back through Old French and Italian to the Arabic "awariya", meaning damaged goods. When a storm ruined part of a ship's cargo, the loss was shared out evenly among all the merchants, and that fair split of a total is exactly what a mean does today.

Three summaries, one question each. The mean is the fair share, the total split evenly across the count, so it feels every value and gets tugged hard by an outlier. The median is the middle of the ordered list, and because it only cares about position it stays honest when the data is lopsided. The mode is the most common value, the right tool for categories and for deciding what is typical to stock or expect. Pick the summary that answers the question you actually asked. And when the mean and median disagree by a lot, do not just average them, that gap is the data telling you it is skewed, with a long tail dragging the mean away from the middle.

review.x: Find the mean of the four numbers \(14\), \(9\), \(22\), and \(15\). Give the number.
hints: Add all four numbers to get the total, then divide by how many numbers there are, which is \(4\).
review.sol: The mean is the total of the values divided by how many values there are. First add them, \(14 + 9 + 22 + 15 = 60\). There are \(4\) numbers, so divide the total by \(4\), $$\text{mean} = \frac{60}{4} = \boxed{15}.$$ The mean of the four numbers is \(15\).

review.x: Find the median of the data set \(7, 2, 9, 4, 6\). Give the number.
hints: Put the five values in order from smallest to largest first, then look for the value sitting right in the middle with two numbers on each side of it.
review.sol: The median is the middle value once the data is in order, so start by sorting the five numbers from smallest to largest, giving \(2, 4, 6, 7, 9\). With five values there is a single middle position, the third one, with two numbers below it and two above it. $$2,\ 4,\ \boxed{6},\ 7,\ 9$$ The value in that center spot is \(6\), so the median is \(6\).

review.x: Find the mode of this data set: \(3, 7, 3, 9, 3, 7, 5\). Give the number.
hints: The mode is the value that appears most often, so tally how many times each number shows up and pick the one with the highest count.
review.sol: The mode is the value that occurs most frequently, so count how often each number appears. Tallying the seven values gives \(3\) three times, \(7\) twice, and \(9\) and \(5\) once each. The winner is clear, since $$3 \text{ appears } 3 \text{ times},$$ more than any other value. The mode is \(\boxed{3}\).

review.x: Find the mean of the four numbers \(10\), \(13\), \(14\), and \(10\). Give your answer as a decimal.
hints: Add all four numbers to get the total, then divide by \(4\) since there are four values. The quotient is not whole, so carry it out as a decimal.
review.sol: The mean is the total of the values divided by how many there are. Adding the four numbers gives \(10 + 13 + 14 + 10 = 47\), and there are \(4\) of them, so divide the total by \(4\), $$\text{mean} = \frac{47}{4} = \boxed{11.75}.$$ The mean of the four numbers is \(11.75\).

review.x: A weather station takes five daily temperature readings, and their mean is \(20\) degrees. Four of the readings are \(15\), \(22\), \(18\), and \(25\). What is the fifth reading? Give the number.
hints: The mean is the total divided by how many values there are, so if five readings average \(20\), their sum must be \(5 \times 20 = 100\). Add up the four readings you know, then see what is left to reach \(100\).
review.sol:
Work backward from the mean. Since the mean of five readings is \(20\), the five readings together must add to \(5 \times 20 = 100\). The four known readings sum to \(15 + 22 + 18 + 25 = 80\), so the fifth reading is whatever fills the gap up to \(100\), giving
$$100 - 80 = \boxed{20}.$$
The fifth reading is \(20\) degrees.

review.x: Find the median of these six numbers: \(12, 4, 8, 20, 6, 10\). Give the number.
hints: Sort the six numbers from smallest to largest first. With an even count there is no single middle value, so the median is the mean of the two middle numbers, the \(3\)rd and \(4\)th in the sorted list.

review.x: On a test, a group of \(10\) students averaged \(80\) points and a separate group of \(15\) students averaged \(90\) points. What is the average score of all \(25\) students combined? Give the number.
hints: The overall average is the total of all points divided by the total number of students, not the average of \(80\) and \(90\). Find the total points each group scored by multiplying its average by its size, then add those totals and divide by \(25\).
review.sol:
This is a weighted mean, so you cannot just average \(80\) and \(90\), because the two groups are different sizes. Instead, rebuild the total score of each group from its average. The \(10\) students who averaged \(80\) scored \(10 \times 80 = 800\) points all together, and the \(15\) students who averaged \(90\) scored \(15 \times 90 = 1350\) points. Add those to get the total for everyone, then divide by all \(25\) students, giving
$$\frac{800 + 1350}{25} = \frac{2150}{25} = \boxed{86}.$$
The combined average is \(86\), which sits closer to \(90\) than to \(80\) because the larger group pulls the mean toward its score.

review.x: A data set has the values \(8\), \(10\), \(9\), \(11\), and \(62\). The mean of these five numbers is \(20\), and the median is \(10\). By how much does the mean exceed the median? Give the number.
hints: The mean and median are both handed to you, so you only need their difference. Sort the values to see why the median is \(10\): the ordered list is \(8, 9, 10, 11, 62\), and the middle value is \(10\), while the lone large value \(62\) drags the mean up to \(20\).
review.sol: Subtract the median from the mean to measure how far apart the two centers sit. The mean of the five values is \(\frac{8 + 10 + 9 + 11 + 62}{5} = \frac{100}{5} = 20\), and sorting the list to \(8, 9, 10, 11, 62\) puts \(10\) in the middle, so the median is \(10\). The single value \(62\) is an outlier that pulls the mean far above the typical value while leaving the median untouched, which is exactly why the two differ. Taking the difference gives $$20 - 10 = \boxed{10}.$$ The mean exceeds the median by \(10\).

review.x: The ten multiples of \(3\) from \(3\) up to \(30\) are \(3, 6, 9, \dots, 30\), and they add up to \(165\). Remove one number so that the nine numbers left have a mean of \(16\). Which number is removed? Give the number.
hints: The nine remaining numbers have a mean of \(16\), so their total must be \(9 \times 16 = 144\). The number you remove is whatever is missing from the original sum of \(165\) to leave that total.
review.sol: The mean of the nine remaining numbers is their sum divided by \(9\), so if that mean is \(16\), the nine numbers must add to \(9 \times 16 = 144\). All ten numbers together add to \(165\), so the removed number is the gap between those two totals, giving $$165 - 144 = \boxed{21}.$$ Since \(21\) is one of the multiples of \(3\) in the list, removing it drops the sum from \(165\) to \(144\) and leaves the nine numbers averaging exactly \(16\). The number removed is \(21\).

review.x: Six different positive whole numbers have a mean of \(10\), so they add up to \(60\). What is the largest that any single one of them can be? Give the number.
hints: To make one number as large as possible while the total stays fixed at \(60\), squeeze every other number as small as it can go. The five others must be different positive whole numbers, so the smallest they can be is \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5\).
review.sol:
The six numbers are fixed to sum to \(60\), since a mean of \(10\) across six values means \(6 \times 10 = 60\). To push one value as high as possible, make the other five as small as they can be. They have to be different positive whole numbers, so the smallest five distinct choices are \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5\), which add to \(15\). Whatever is left over goes to the sixth number, giving

$$60 - 15 = \boxed{45}.$$

The largest any single one of them can be is \(45\).

review.x: The mean of five numbers is \(12\). A sixth number is added, and the mean of all six numbers rises to \(14\). What is the sixth number? Give the number.
hints: The mean times the count gives the total, so the five numbers add to \(5 \times 12 = 60\) and all six add to \(6 \times 14 = 84\). The sixth number is whatever fills the gap between those two totals.
review.sol: Turn each mean back into a total by multiplying the mean by how many numbers it covers. The first five numbers have mean \(12\), so their total is \(5 \times 12 = 60\). After the sixth number is added, all six have mean \(14\), so their total is \(6 \times 14 = 84\). The sixth number is the difference between the new total and the old one, giving $$84 - 60 = \boxed{24}.$$ So the sixth number is \(24\).

review.x: A shoe shop sold these sizes over one afternoon: \(7, 8, 8, 9, 8, 10, 7, 8\). The owner wants to restock the most common size, since that is what customers buy the most. Which size should the shop restock the most of? Give the size.
hints: The size to restock is the one that sold the most, which is the mode. Count how many times each size appears and find the one with the highest count.
review.sol: The most common value in a data set is the mode, so restocking the size that sold most means finding the mode. Tally each size: \(7\) appears twice, \(9\) appears once, \(10\) appears once, and \(8\) appears in four of the eight sales. No other size comes close, so the mode is $$\boxed{8}.$$ The shop should restock size \(8\), because it was the size customers bought most often.

review.x: Find the average of all the whole numbers from \(41\) to \(59\), including both ends. Give the number.
hints: When a list of numbers is evenly spaced, you do not have to add them all up. The mean sits right in the middle, so average the smallest and largest values, \((41+59)/2\).
review.sol: The numbers \(41, 42, 43, \dots, 59\) are evenly spaced, one apart, so they are perfectly balanced around their center. For any evenly spaced list the mean equals the average of the first and last value, which saves you from adding all nineteen numbers. Averaging the two ends gives $$\frac{41+59}{2}=\frac{100}{2}=\boxed{50}.$$ The average of the whole numbers from \(41\) to \(59\) is \(50\).

review.x: A set of five positive whole numbers has a mean of \(6\), a median of \(6\), and \(8\) as its only mode. What is the difference between the largest and smallest values in the set? Give the number.
hints: The mean of \(6\) over five numbers forces the total to be \(30\), and the median of \(6\) fixes the middle value as \(6\), so line the five numbers up from smallest to largest and pin down the rest so that \(8\) shows up more than any other value while the whole list still sums to \(30\).
review.sol:
Work from the three clues one at a time. A mean of \(6\) across five numbers means they add to \(5 \times 6 = 30\). A median of \(6\) means the middle value, the third when the numbers are sorted, equals \(6\). For \(8\) to be the only mode it must appear more than any other value, so the two largest slots are both \(8\), giving \(6, 8, 8\) for the top three and a running total of \(22\). That leaves the two smallest numbers to add to \(30 - 22 = 8\), and they must be different from each other and from \(8\) so no value ties \(8\) for most frequent. The pair \(3\) and \(5\) works, so the set is \(3, 5, 6, 8, 8\). The largest value is \(8\) and the smallest is \(3\), giving
$$8 - 3 = \boxed{5}.$$
The difference between the largest and smallest values is \(5\).

blocks.x: In 10.6 you squeezed a whole list down to one number with the mean, median, and mode. Now we go the other way and read data that someone has already arranged for you. A good display shows at a glance what a bare list hides, the biggest category, a trend over time, how a whole splits into parts. In this lesson you will read tables, bar and line graphs, pie charts, stem-and-leaf plots, and histograms.

The table above shows how many boxes of cookies three scout troops sold in October and November. Birch's Total is highlighted with a ? because it is missing. Complete the table by finding Birch's Total. Give the number.
hints: A Total is just the two months added together, so find Birch's row and look only at its October and November cells.
Read across Birch's row from left to right, then add the October number to the November number to fill in the ? cell.
blocks.sol: Read across Birch's row and pick out its October and November boxes. The Total for a troop is the two months added, so add them together, giving $$120 + 65 = \boxed{185}.$$ Birch sold 185 boxes in all.

blocks.x: Tables. A table sorts data into rows and columns so any single value is easy to find. To read one, run your finger along a row and down a column until they meet, and there sits the exact number you want, pulled clean out of a pile of others without hunting. Many tables add a Total row or a Total column that sums a whole line at once. When you need a total, read straight across the row and add every entry, or read straight down the column and add, and if the total is already given you just read it off. That is the power of a table, exact lookup. Every value has one home, so you always know where to look.
The bar graph above shows how many marbles of each color are in a jar. Which color is most common, and how many marbles of that color are there? Give the number.
hints: The most common color is the one with the tallest bar. Scan across all five bars and find the one that reaches highest.
Once you have spotted the tallest bar, trace from its top straight over to the number scale on the left to read its height.
blocks.sol: Read the height of each bar and look for the tallest one. The blue bar rises higher than red, green, yellow, and purple, so blue is the most common color. Tracing the top of the blue bar across to the scale gives its count, so $$\boxed{22}$$ The jar holds more blue marbles than any other color, and there are 22 of them.

blocks.x: The bar graph above shows how many goals five players scored. How many more goals did the top scorer make than the lowest scorer? Give the number.
hints: Find the tallest bar and the shortest bar. The tallest tells you the top scorer's goals, the shortest tells you the lowest scorer's goals.
Once you have those two numbers, subtract the smaller from the larger to see how many more goals the top scorer made.
blocks.sol:
Read the tallest bar to find the top scorer and the shortest bar to find the lowest scorer. The tallest bar reaches 8 and the shortest reaches 3. Subtract to find how many more the top scorer made, giving
$$8 - 3 = \boxed{5}.$$
The top scorer made 5 more goals than the lowest scorer.

blocks.x: Bar graphs. A bar graph turns amounts into heights, so a taller bar means more and you can compare categories at a glance without reading a single number. To pull out an actual value, read the top of a bar straight across to the axis. Once every height is a number, the usual arithmetic works. The tallest bar is the most, the gap between two bars is a difference, and all the bars added together give a total.

The line graph above tracks a plant's height in centimeters across six weeks. During which week did the plant climb the most from the week before, meaning the week with the steepest rise on the graph? Give the week number.
hints: The biggest growth is the steepest part of the line, where it climbs fastest from one dot to the next. Scan across the graph and find where the jump between two neighboring weeks looks largest.
To be sure, work out each week-to-week change by subtracting the earlier height from the next one. The week you want is the one where that difference is the biggest, and you name the later week.
blocks.sol:
Read the height at each week off the line graph and find the biggest jump from one week to the next. Subtracting each height from the one after it gives the weekly gains, and the largest of these is the climb into week 4,
$$16 - 9 = 7 \text{ centimeters}.$$
No other week rises by more than 7, so the plant grew the most going into week \(\boxed{4}\).

blocks.x: The line graph above tracks two food trucks over five days. On exactly one day the two trucks sold the same amount, which is where the lines meet. How many did each truck sell on that day? Give the number.
hints: The two lines start apart and end apart, but there is one spot where they cross. Find the day where they touch at the same point.
Once you spot where the lines meet, slide straight across to the vertical axis and read off the height at that shared point.
blocks.sol:
Look for the day where the two lines cross, since that is the only day both trucks match. The lines meet on day 3. Slide across from that crossing point to the vertical axis to read its height, giving
$$\boxed{45}.$$
Both trucks sold 45 on the day their lines meet.

blocks.x: Line graphs. A line graph is built for change over time. The line's steepness is the rate, so a steep climb is fast growth and a flat stretch is no change at all. That means the biggest jump lives in the steepest segment, not the highest point. When two lines are drawn together, the place where they cross is the moment the two quantities are equal, and the vertical gap between them is how far apart they are.
The pie chart above shows the favorite season of 400 surveyed students. How many of them chose summer? Give the number.
hints: Find the summer slice on the pie chart above and read the percent printed on it. That percent tells you the share of the 400 students who picked summer.
To turn a percent of a group into a count, multiply the percent (as a decimal) by the total number of students. So take summer's percent, write it as a decimal, and multiply by 400.
blocks.sol:
Read the summer slice on the pie chart above. It is labeled \(40\%\), so 40 percent of the 400 students chose summer. Turn the percent into a decimal and multiply by the total, giving
$$0.40 \times 400 = \boxed{160}.$$
So 160 students chose summer.

blocks.x: The pie chart above shows the favorite season of 400 students. How many more students chose summer than winter? Give the number.
hints: Summer is the biggest slice and winter is one of the smallest. You can subtract the percents first, finding summer's percent minus winter's percent, then take that percent of 400.
Or find each count on its own by multiplying its percent by 400, then subtract the winter count from the summer count.
blocks.sol:
Read summer and winter off the pie chart above. Summer is 40 percent of 400 and winter is 15 percent of 400. One quick way is to subtract the percents first, then take that share of 400, giving

$$(0.40 - 0.15)\times 400 = 0.25 \times 400 = 100.$$

Counting each group instead, summer is \(0.40\times 400 = 160\) students and winter is \(0.15\times 400 = 60\), so \(160 - 60 = \boxed{100}\). Either way, 100 more students chose summer than winter.

blocks.x: The pie chart above shows students' favorite pets, but the reptile slice is left blank with a question mark. What percent is the reptile slice? Give the number.
hints: Every slice of a pie chart together covers the whole circle, so all the percents have to add up to 100.
Add the four percents the chart already labels, then see how much is left over to reach 100.
blocks.sol:
The whole pie is 100 percent, so the five slices must add to 100. Add the four labeled slices and subtract from 100 to find the reptile slice, giving
$$100-(35+25+15+12)=100-87=\boxed{13}.$$
The reptile slice is 13 percent.

blocks.x: Pie charts. A pie chart shows how a whole splits into parts, and because the circle is the whole, all the slices must add to 100%. That gives you two moves. To turn a slice into a count, take its percent of the total, so a slice worth \(25\%\) of a group of \(80\) is $$0.25 \times 80 = 20.$$ To find a missing slice, subtract the known slices from 100%. A pie is the right display when you care about shares of a whole, not raw amounts.
The stem-and-leaf plot above shows how many push-ups each of thirteen students did. What is the median number of push-ups? Give the number.
hints: The leaves in a stem-and-leaf plot are already listed in order from smallest to largest, so you can just count through the values as they appear from the top row down.
With thirteen values, the median is the middle one, the 7th value counting up from the smallest. Count to the 7th leaf and read its stem and leaf together.
blocks.sol:
The median is the middle value, and a stem-and-leaf plot already lists the leaves in order. With thirteen push-up counts, the middle one is the 7th value counting from the smallest.

Counting through the leaves from the top, the 7th value lands in the stem-2 row, on the leaf 7, giving

$$\text{median} = \boxed{27}$$

So the median number of push-ups is 27.

blocks.x: The stem-and-leaf plot above shows eleven quiz scores. Find the mode of the scores. Give the number.
hints: The mode is the value that shows up most often. On a stem-and-leaf plot, that means hunting for the leaf that repeats the most on a single stem.
Scan each stem for a leaf written more than once, then rebuild the full score by joining that stem to its repeated leaf the way the key shows.
blocks.sol:
The mode is the value that appears most often, so look for the leaf that repeats the most on one stem. On stem 6, the leaf 8 is written three times, more than any other leaf on any stem. Reading that stem and leaf together the way the key does gives

$$6\,|\,8 = \boxed{68}.$$

The score that appears most often is 68.

blocks.x: Stem-and-leaf plots. A stem-and-leaf plot splits each number into a stem, the leading digits, and a leaf, the last digit. So the value \(47\) becomes a stem of \(4\) and a leaf of \(7\). All the numbers with the same stem share one row, and their leaves get listed out beside it. That gives you the shape of the data like a sideways bar graph, since a longer row of leaves means more values landed in that range, while still keeping every exact number. And because the leaves in each row are written in order from smallest to largest, the plot does double duty. You can find the median by counting leaves inward to the middle one, and the mode by spotting the leaf value that repeats most.
The histogram above shows how many households own each number of pets. How many households were surveyed in all? Give the number.
hints: Each bar's height tells you how many households fall into that pet count, so no single bar is the whole survey.
To count everyone once, add the heights of all the bars together.
blocks.sol: Every household lands in exactly one bar, so the total surveyed is the sum of all the bar heights. Reading each bar's height off the vertical axis and adding them, $$5+12+8+3+2=\boxed{30}.$$ So 30 households were surveyed in all.

blocks.x: The histogram above shows how many households own each number of pets. What is the mean number of pets per household? Give your answer as a decimal.
hints: A mean is the total number of pets divided by the total number of households. Read the height of each bar to get how many homes fall in that group, and add those heights to find the total households.
To get the total pets, multiply each pet count by the number of households at that bar, then add all those products. That handles the fact that some counts happen far more often than others.
blocks.sol:
Read each bar's height to see how many households own that many pets, then build a weighted total. Multiply each pet count by its number of households, add those up, and divide by the total households. There are \(5+12+8+3+2=30\) households, so

$$\frac{0\cdot5+1\cdot12+2\cdot8+3\cdot3+4\cdot2}{30}=\frac{45}{30}=\boxed{1.5}$$

The mean is 1.5 pets per household.

blocks.x: Histograms. A histogram is a bar graph of frequency, how OFTEN each value or range shows up. That is why its bars touch, with no gaps, since the values sweep across a continuous number line. The tallest bar marks the value that happens most, so it is the mode. Add up all the bar heights and you get the total count. And the mean is the same combined-mean move from 10.6, weighting each value by its frequency, add those products, and divide by the total, $$\text{mean}=\frac{\text{sum of (value}\times\text{frequency)}}{\text{total count}}.$$
The bar graph above shows the daily high temperature for six days, Monday through Saturday. What was the median daily high over those six days? Give the number.
hints: Read the height of every bar first, all six of them, then write the temperatures in order from lowest to highest.
With six values there is no single middle bar. Find the two temperatures sitting in the middle of your ordered list and average them.
blocks.sol:
Read all six bar heights and put them in order from lowest to highest, giving \(12, 15, 16, 18, 20, 22\). Six values is an even count, so the median is the average of the two middle ones, the 3rd and 4th in the list. Those are \(16\) and \(18\), so average them,
$$\frac{16+18}{2}=\frac{34}{2}=\boxed{17}$$
The median daily high temperature over the six days is 17 degrees.

blocks.label: Fun fact
blocks.x: Almost every graph you have read in this lesson was dreamed up by one restless Scottish engineer. William Playfair published the first bar chart and the first line graphs in his 1786 Commercial and Political Atlas, and in 1801 he drew the very first pie chart. Before him, numbers lived only in long tables and columns, and the idea of turning quantity into a picture you could take in at a glance was brand new. So when you find the tallest bar or trace a line to its peak, you are using tools that trace back to Playfair, who figured out that the eye can often see a pattern the eye would never spot in a wall of digits.

Match the display to the question. Every display in this lesson is a tool built for one kind of question, and the whole skill is choosing the one that makes your question easy to answer. Reach for a table when you need an exact value or a total. You look up one cell or read across a row to the Total column, and the number is right there, no eyeballing. Reach for a bar graph to compare amounts across categories. The tallest bar wins and the shortest loses, and you can see the gap between any two at a glance. Reach for a line graph to track change over time, or to find where two lines cross. A rising line means going up, a falling line means going down, and a crossing point is the moment two things are equal. Reach for a pie chart for shares of a whole. Every slice is a percent, all the slices add to $$100\%,$$ and to turn a slice into a count you take that percent of the total. Reach for a stem-and-leaf plot when you want to keep every single value and still see the shape. The middle leaf gives the median and the most repeated leaf gives the mode, both just by reading. Reach for a histogram to see how often values occur. The tallest bar is the mode, and to find the mean you weight each value by how many times it happens. So the move is always the same. Decide what the question is really asking, pick the display that answers it cleanly, then read it carefully.

review.x: The table above shows the points each team scored in two rounds. Add up every entry to find the grand total of all points scored across both rounds. Give the number.
hints: Read the whole table, not one row. Every cell holds points that were actually scored, so add all six entries together. It helps to total each round column first, then add those two column totals.
review.sol:
Add every number in the table. Total each round column first, then combine. Round 1 gives \(30+41+28=99\), and Round 2 gives \(22+19+35=76\). Now add the two column totals, so

$$99+76=\boxed{175}.$$

The grand total of all points scored is 175.

review.x: The bar graph above shows how many votes each music genre received in a class poll. How many votes did rock get? Give the number.
hints: Find the rock bar and trace its top across to the scale on the side. The height where it lands is the number of votes.
review.sol: Read the height of the rock bar against the vote scale. Its top lines up with the 18 mark, giving $$\boxed{18}$$ votes. So rock received 18 votes.

review.x: The bar graph above shows how many new members the club gained each quarter. How many new members did the club gain over the whole year? Give the number.
hints: The whole year is all four quarters together, so read the height of each bar and add the four numbers.
review.sol: Read the height of each bar and add them, since the year is all four quarters together, giving $$9+13+7+11=\boxed{40}.$$ The club gained 40 new members over the whole year.

review.x: The line graph above tracks a seedling's height in centimeters over six weeks. How tall was the seedling at week 4? Give the number.
hints: Find week 4 on the bottom axis, follow that column straight up to the plotted point, and read its height off the left axis.
review.sol: Go to week 4 on the horizontal axis and look at the point plotted there. Reading straight across to the height axis lands you at $$\boxed{14}$$ so the seedling was 14 centimeters tall at week 4.

review.x: The line graph above tracks two runners' weekly miles over five weeks. On how many of the five weeks did Runner A run more miles than Runner B? Give the number.
hints: Go week by week and check whose point sits higher. Runner A wins a week only when A's dot is above B's dot, so count just those weeks.
review.sol: Walk across the graph one week at a time and mark each week where Runner A's point sits above Runner B's. That happens in week 2, week 3, and week 5, and B is higher or tied on the other weeks. Counting the weeks where A is on top, $$3 \text{ weeks} = \boxed{3}$$ So Runner A ran more miles than Runner B on 3 of the five weeks.

review.x: The pie chart above shows how 300 students get to school. How many of them take the bus? Give the number.
hints: The bus slice is a percent of the whole group. To turn a percent into a count, multiply that percent (as a decimal) by the total number of students.
review.sol:
Read the bus slice off the pie chart above, then take that percent of the 300 students. Writing the percent as a decimal and multiplying gives
$$0.45 \times 300 = \boxed{135}.$$
So 135 students take the bus.

review.x: The pie chart above shows how the 300 students get to school. How many students either walk or bike? Add those two slices, then find that percent of 300. Give the number.
hints: Read the two slices for walking and biking off the pie chart, add their percents to get one combined percent, then take that percent of 300 by multiplying.
review.sol:
Read the walk and bike slices from the pie chart and add them, so the combined share is \(20\% + 10\% = 30\%\). Now take \(30\%\) of the 300 students, giving

$$0.30 \times 300 = \boxed{90}.$$

So 90 students walk or bike.

review.x: The pie chart above shows the favorite colors in a class. One slice, orange, is marked with a question mark instead of its percent. What percent of the class picked orange? What percent?
hints: Every slice of a pie chart adds up to 100 percent, so read the four labeled percents, add them, and see what is left over for the orange slice.
review.sol: A whole pie chart is 100 percent. Add the four labeled slices, then take that away from 100 to find what orange must be. Adding the known slices gives $$28 + 22 + 19 + 16 = 85,$$ so the orange slice is the leftover, $$100 - 85 = \boxed{15}.$$ Orange is 15 percent of the class.

review.x: The stem-and-leaf plot above shows the eight scores from a bowling match. What is the median of these scores? Give the number.
hints: Read the plot from top to bottom to list the scores in order, smallest to largest. Since there are eight scores, the median sits between the two middle ones, so find those two values and average them.
review.sol: Read the leaves off each stem in order, which already gives the eight scores from smallest to largest. With eight values the median is the mean of the fourth and fifth scores, which are 31 and 33. Average them, giving $$\frac{31+33}{2}=\boxed{32}.$$ The median score is 32.

review.x: The stem-and-leaf plot above shows ten step-counts, in hundreds. How many days had 40 hundred steps or more? How many days?
hints: A value of 40 hundred or more means the stem is 4 or 5. Look only at those two rows and count every leaf sitting on them.
review.sol:
You want values of at least 40 hundred, so read the stems that start those values, which are stem 4 and stem 5. Stem 4 carries three leaves and stem 5 carries one, giving

$$3 + 1 = \boxed{4}$$

So 4 days had 40 hundred steps or more.

review.x: The histogram above shows how many students have each number of siblings. What is the most common number of siblings among these students? Give the number.
hints: The most common value is the one shared by the most students, so find the tallest bar and read the number of siblings it sits over on the bottom axis.
review.sol: Look for the tallest bar in the histogram, since its height tells you how many students share that number of siblings. The bar over 2 siblings rises higher than every other bar, so more students have 2 siblings than any other amount. That makes the most common number of siblings $$\boxed{2}.$$ The mode is 2 siblings.

review.x: The histogram above shows how many books each student read last month. How many students were surveyed in all? Give the number.
hints: Each bar's height is the number of students in that group, so the total number surveyed is the sum of all the bar heights added together.
review.sol:
Every student counted lands in exactly one bar, so add up all the bar heights to get the total surveyed. Reading the bars from left to right and summing their heights,

$$3+7+8+5+1=\boxed{24}.$$

So 24 students were surveyed in all.

review.x: The pie chart above shows how 500 smoothie orders split across four flavors. How many more mango smoothies were ordered than kiwi? Give the number.
hints: Each slice is a percent of the 500 orders, so turn the mango slice and the kiwi slice into counts by multiplying each percent by 500, then subtract the smaller count from the larger.
review.sol:
Read the mango slice and the kiwi slice off the pie chart above, then turn each percent into a count out of 500. Mango is \(40\%\) of 500, so \(0.40 \times 500 = 200\) orders. Kiwi is \(15\%\) of 500, so \(0.15 \times 500 = 75\) orders. Now subtract to compare, giving
$$200 - 75 = \boxed{125}.$$
So 125 more mango smoothies were ordered than kiwi.

blocks.x: You can now find the mean, the median, and the mode of any list of numbers. This lesson turns that skill around and asks a harder question, what those single numbers do not tell you. One statistic is a summary, and a summary always leaves something out. Sometimes what it hides is small. Sometimes it is the whole story.

Two basketball teams both average 20 points a game. The Hawks scored 19, 20, and 21 points in their three games. The Falcons scored 5, 20, and 35. Find the range of the Falcons' scores, meaning their highest score minus their lowest score. Give the number.
hints: Both teams share the same mean, so the average alone tells you nothing about how the three games differed. Look instead at how far apart the Falcons' scores stretch.
The range measures spread, not center. Subtract the smallest Falcons score from the largest and see how much wider it is than anything the Hawks did.
blocks.sol: The mean is a center, and it says nothing about spread. Both teams average 20 points, yet the Hawks stayed near 19, 20, 21 while the Falcons swung from a cold 5 to a hot 35. To measure that swing, take the range, the highest score minus the lowest, giving $$35 - 5 = \boxed{30}.$$ The Falcons' range is 30 while the Hawks' is only 2, so two teams with the exact same average can be worlds apart in consistency. The mean hid the whole story of how wildly the Falcons' games varied.

blocks.x: <svg viewBox="0 0 560 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Two number lines. Both sets of dots have the same mean of twenty, marked with a gold line. The top set is tightly clustered near twenty; the bottom set is spread far out, yet both share the identical mean."><line x1="60" y1="70" x2="520" y2="70" stroke="#7c8aa0" stroke-width="2" blocks.cap: Both sets of scores average 20, yet the top row huddles right around 20 while the bottom row flings out to 4 and 36. The same mean can rest on tight data or on wild data, so the average by itself never reveals the spread.

blocks.x: The center hides the spread. Two lists can share the exact same mean and still be worlds apart, one bunched tightly together and one flung wide. Picture the quiz scores \(79, 80, 81\) next to \(30, 80, 130\). Both average to $$\frac{79+80+81}{3}=\frac{30+80+130}{3}=80,$$ yet the first group barely moved off center while the second swung from near zero to well past a hundred. The mean marks a center, but it says nothing about how far the values scatter around that center. Spread, how consistent or how varied the data is, is a separate fact the average quietly leaves out.

Anna took seven quizzes and scored 94, 93, 90, 93, 92, and 91. She missed the last quiz and scored a 0. Because of that single 0, her mean sits at about 79, which makes her look like a middling student. Find the median of her seven scores, the summary number that shrugs off the lone 0. Give the number.
hints: The mean got dragged down because it adds in the 0 and shares the damage across every score. The median works differently, so ask yourself what the median actually looks at.
Put all seven scores in order from lowest to highest. The median is the single value sitting exactly in the middle, so the one stray 0 at the bottom of the list barely gets a vote.
blocks.sol: The mean is fragile because it lets one extreme value, an outlier, pull the whole average toward it. The 0 does exactly that here, dropping the mean to about 79. The median is sturdier because it only cares about position, not size. \nOrder the seven scores from lowest to highest, giving $$0,\ 90,\ 91,\ 92,\ 93,\ 93,\ 94.$$\nWith seven values the median is the 4th one, the value with three scores on each side, so $$\boxed{92}.$$ \nThe median is 92, while the mean is only about 79. Notice how differently they describe Anna. The mean says "average student" because the 0 got baked into the total, but the median says "strong student" because it steps right past that single missed quiz. The answer is 92, and the lesson is that a mean can hide a story that one outlier is quietly rewriting, while the median tells you what a typical quiz really looked like.

blocks.x: Outliers wreck the mean, not the median. The mean adds up every value, so one number far from the rest drags the whole average toward itself. Slip a single wild value into a data set and the mean lurches after it, while the median barely notices, because the median only cares about which value lands in the middle. Take the ages of four kids and their teacher, \(10\), \(11\), \(12\), \(13\), and \(45\). The median age is $$\text{median} = 12,$$ a fair picture of the group. The mean tells a different story, giving $$\text{mean} = \frac{10+11+12+13+45}{5} = \frac{91}{5} = 18.2,$$ an age older than every student in the room. So when one value is dramatically different from the others, trust the median. It reports what is typical, while the mean reports what one outlier dragged it toward.
A basketball player's five games scored 40, 45, 50, 55, and 110 points. Find the mean and the median of these five scores, then give the mean minus the median. Give the number.
hints: Line the five scores up in order and pick the middle one for the median, then add all five and divide by five for the mean. Notice they will not come out equal.
One score, the 110, sits far above the rest. Think about which statistic that giant number drags upward and which one it barely touches.
blocks.sol: The gap between the mean and the median is a skew detector. The median is the middle of the sorted scores 40, 45, 50, 55, 110, which is 50. The mean adds all five and divides by five, giving $$\frac{40+45+50+55+110}{5}=\frac{300}{5}=60.$$ So the mean minus the median is $$60-50=\boxed{10}.$$ The single huge game of 110 barely moves the median, since the middle spot stays at 50, but it pulls the mean all the way up to 60. A positive gap like this is a warning sign that a few unusually large values are stretching the average. The mean alone would make every game look like a 60-point night, hiding the fact that four of the five were far below that.

median</text></svg>
blocks.cap: When a few values stretch far in one direction, the mean gets pulled toward that long tail while the median stays put inside the crowded cluster. The gap between the two is what points to the skew.

blocks.x: Compare the mean and median. Line the two numbers up next to each other and the gap between them tells a story. When the mean sits above the median, a few unusually large values are stretching a long tail out to the right. Incomes work this way, where a handful of enormous salaries drag the mean up while most people earn far less. When the mean sits below the median, a few unusually small values pull a tail out to the left. When the two are close, nothing is pulling hard in either direction and the data is roughly balanced. So the size and direction of the gap is a quick detector for lopsided data and hidden outliers, $$\text{mean} - \text{median} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{tail to the right},$$ and a gap near zero means the summary number is safe to trust.

A basketball team of 11 players has a median height of 76 inches. Someone claims the tallest player must therefore be well above 76 inches. What is the shortest the tallest player could possibly be? Give the number in inches.
hints: The median is the height of the 6th player once you line all 11 up in order. The five players above that spot are each at least as tall as the median, and the tallest is the very top of that group. So all you know for sure is that the tallest is at least 76 inches.

Ask yourself whether anything forces the top players to be strictly taller. Ties are allowed, so imagine the whole top half measuring exactly 76 inches. That is a legal team with a median of 76, and its tallest player is 76 too.
blocks.sol:
The median pins down the middle value and says nothing definite about how far the top stretches. Line the 11 heights up from shortest to tallest. The median is the 6th height, which is 76 inches, so the five players above it are each at least 76 inches. That makes the tallest at least 76 inches as well.

But nothing forces those top players to be strictly taller, because ties are fine. Picture the whole upper half sitting at exactly 76.

$$76,\ 76,\ 76,\ 76,\ 76,\ \underbrace{76}_{\text{median}},\ 76,\ 76,\ 76,\ 76,\ 76$$

The median is still 76, and now the tallest player is 76 too, so the shortest the tallest player could possibly be is $$\boxed{76}\text{ inches.}$$

The lesson is that the median hides the tails. It reports the middle and leaves the extremes free, so a median of 76 is fully consistent with a tallest player of exactly 76 or of 90. A single middle number tells you nothing certain about how tall the tallest player really is.

blocks.x: The median is silent about the extremes. The median pins down the middle value and nothing more. It cannot tell you how high the highest reaches or how low the lowest sinks. Say a basketball team has a median height of 76 inches, so the middle player stands at 76 inches. That is all you know for certain. The tallest player could also be exactly 76 inches, with everyone bunched near the middle, or the tallest could tower at 90 inches. Both worlds have the same median, because everything above the middle is free to be anything at or beyond it. The median guards the center and lets the edges roam.

Five houses on a block are worth 10, 14, 20, 26, and 30 in ten-thousands of dollars, so their median value is 20. A developer builds a sixth house on the block, a mansion worth 5000 in the same units (fifty million dollars). Find the new median of all six houses, in ten-thousands of dollars. Give the number.
hints: The median only cares about the position of the middle value, not how far the largest number sits from the others. With six houses now, there is no single middle, so the median is the average of the two central values once everything is in order.
Sort all six values and find the two that land in the middle. The giant mansion sits at the far end, so it never becomes a middle value, and it can only push the median a little.
blocks.sol: This shows how the median resists an extreme value. Sort the six house values: 10, 14, 20, 26, 30, 5000. With an even count, the median is the average of the two middle numbers, which are 20 and 26, giving $$\frac{20 + 26}{2} = \boxed{23}.$$ The mansion is worth fifty million dollars, yet the median only nudged from 20 to 23. The mean, by contrast, would rocket past 800, dragged up by that one huge number. So the median barely felt the mansion, while a single summary number like the mean would hide the fact that five ordinary houses share the block with one giant. The answer is 23.
