blocks.cap: Six values sit clustered together, and then one point is dropped far off to the right. The median steps over by just one value, while the arrow shows the mean lunging toward that lone far point, which is exactly why the median stays the steadier summary.

blocks.x: A class of 20 students took a test scored out of 100, and their mean was exactly 75. Nothing else about the individual scores is known. Consider these four statements. (1) At least one student scored 75 or higher. (2) The twenty scores add up to 1500. (3) At least one student scored below 75. (4) No one scored a perfect 100. How many of the four statements must be true? Give the number.
hints: A mean of 75 pins down the total, since the total is always the mean times the count. But knowing the total tells you nothing about how any single score is spread around 75.

For each statement, try to build a class that breaks it. If you cannot break it, it must be true. Ask yourself whether every score could sit exactly at 75, or whether one score could be jammed all the way up to 100.
blocks.sol:
The trap is thinking the mean tells you more than it does. A mean fixes the total and nothing else, so most claims about individual scores are not forced.

Start with the one fact the mean guarantees. Total equals mean times count, giving
$$75 \times 20 = 1500.$$
So statement (2) is locked in. It must be true.

Now statement (1). If every student scored below 75, the mean would land below 75 too, which contradicts a mean of exactly 75. So someone has to be at 75 or above. It must be true.

Statement (3) sounds symmetric, but it is not forced. Imagine all twenty students scored exactly 75. The total is still 1500 and the mean is still 75, yet nobody scored below 75. So (3) can fail.

Statement (4) is also not forced. One student could score a perfect 100, with the rest arranged to keep the total at 1500. So a perfect score is allowed, and (4) can fail.

Two statements are chained to the mean and two are free, so
$$\boxed{2}$$
of the four must be true. The lesson is that a mean hands you the total and one guaranteed value near it, then goes silent. Everything about the spread, the extremes, and the individual scores stays hidden.

blocks.x: A summary throws information away. A single statistic squeezes a whole list into one number, and that squeeze runs one way. You cannot rebuild the data from its mean, because countless different lists share the very same mean. The scores \(4, 5, 6\) and the scores \(1, 5, 9\) both average to $$\frac{4+5+6}{3} = \frac{1+5+9}{3} = 5,$$ yet one list barely moves while the other swings wide. Knowing an average pins down the total and nothing more. It stays silent about the individual values, how high they climb, how low they fall, and how far apart they sit.

Richville has an average wealth of 150 thousand dollars per person, and Poorville has an average of 20 thousand dollars per person. The two towns merge into one. If Richville and Poorville have exactly the same number of people, what is the merged town's average wealth, in thousands? Give the number.
hints: When two groups have the same number of people, each group's average counts for exactly the same share of the combined total, so the merged average sits right in the middle of the two.
Watch what makes this work. It is the equal sizes. Add the two averages and split the result in half, and ask yourself whether you could still do that if one town were much larger than the other.
blocks.sol: The idea here is that you can only average the averages when the groups are the same size. Because Richville and Poorville have the same number of people, each town contributes an equal share to the merged total, so the two averages get equal weight. That lets you just average them, $$\frac{150 + 20}{2} = \boxed{85}$$ so the merged town's average wealth is 85 thousand dollars per person. The thing to notice is that this shortcut is fragile. It works only because the two towns are the same size. If Poorville had ten times as many people as Richville, the merged average would be pulled far below 85, closer to Poorville's 20. A blended average hides the sizes of the groups behind it, and without those sizes the halfway point can be badly wrong.

blocks.x: Two towns are merging into one. Richville has 3 residents with an average wealth of 150 thousand dollars each. Poorville has 7 residents with an average wealth of 20 thousand dollars each. After the merge the new town has all 10 people. Find the average wealth of the merged town, in thousands. Give the number.
hints: You cannot just average the two town averages, because the towns are not the same size. To find the total wealth of a group, multiply its average by how many people it holds.
Poorville has more than twice as many residents as Richville, so its lower average gets far more say. Add up all the wealth, then divide by all 10 people.
blocks.sol: This is a weighted mean, where each town counts as much as it has people. Averaging the two numbers straight would give the halfway point of 85, but that treats the towns as equal in size when they are not. Richville holds \(3 \times 150 = 450\) thousand in total wealth, and Poorville holds \(7 \times 20 = 140\) thousand. Divide the combined wealth by all 10 residents, giving $$\frac{450 + 140}{10} = \boxed{59}$$ The merged average is 59, which sits far below the midpoint of 85. Poorville has more than twice as many people, so its lower number pulls the average toward itself. A plain average of the averages hides the sizes of the groups, and size is exactly what decides where the true mean lands.

blocks.x: You cannot average averages. To combine two group means into one, you need to know how big each group is. Suppose one class of 30 students averages 70 on a test and another class of 10 students averages 90. It is tempting to say the combined average is \(\frac{70+90}{2}=80\), but that is wrong. Averaging the two means treats the classes as if they were the same size, and here they are not. The right way is to weight each mean by how many values it stands for, so the 30 scores near 70 pull harder than the 10 scores near 90, giving $$\text{combined mean}=\frac{30\times 70 + 10\times 90}{30+10}=\frac{3000}{40}=75.$$ The true average is 75, not 80. It sits closer to 70 because the larger group has more pull. Averaging the averages is only correct in the special case where the groups are equally sized. Whenever they differ, the combined mean drifts toward the average of whichever group is larger, and you have to weight by size to find it.

A company advertises that its "average salary" is 80 thousand dollars, and technically that is true. But that mean is pulled up by a handful of executives who earn far more than everyone else. The median salary is 45 thousand, and nearly every worker earns close to that. Which number honestly describes what a typical worker at this company earns, in thousands? Give the number (in thousands).
hints: A statistic can be true and still misleading. Ask what the number is standing in for here, which is the pay of a normal, in-the-middle worker, and then ask which measure actually sits in the middle.

A few executive salaries are large enough to drag the mean upward, away from where most people actually are. The measure that ignores how extreme those top values get, and just marks the middle of the line, is the one that stays honest.
blocks.sol:
The idea is that a few very large values can inflate the mean, so the mean drifts up above where most people actually sit, while the median stays put at the middle of the pack.

Line everyone up from lowest paid to highest paid. The median is whoever stands in the middle, so it reports what a genuinely typical worker earns, which is 45 thousand. The mean, on the other hand, adds every salary and divides, so the enormous executive salaries get spread across the whole total and lift it. That is how the mean lands at

$$\text{mean} = 80 \text{ thousand}, \qquad \text{median} = \boxed{45} \text{ thousand}.$$

Since almost everyone earns close to 45 thousand, the median is the honest "typical" here. The company quoted the mean because it is the flattering number, not the accurate one. When a small group at the top holds unusually large values, the mean gets pulled toward them and can make pay look better than it really is, so the number that honestly describes a typical worker is 45.

blocks.x: Nine students turned in a project, each scored out of 10. Their scores were 3, 3, 3, 3, 7, 8, 9, 10, and 10. The mode is 3, which makes it sound like the class struggled. Find the median instead, since it better reflects how the class actually did. Give the number.
hints: The mode is just the score that shows up most often. Four students landed on a 3, so it wins the count, but ask yourself whether most of the class was really down there.
The median is the middle value once the scores are lined up in order. With nine scores, count in to the 5th one from either end.
blocks.sol:
The mode reports the most common score, and nothing more. Four students scored 3, so 3 is the mode, but that count of four is the whole story it tells. It says nothing about the other five students.

The median finds the middle of the ordered list. The scores are already in order, and with nine of them the middle is the 5th value, counting in from either end.

$$3,\ 3,\ 3,\ 3,\ \boxed{7},\ 8,\ 9,\ 10,\ 10$$

The median is 7. Look at the top half, 7, 8, 9, 10, 10, and you see most of the class actually scored high. The mode of 3 hid that by rewarding the one cluster of low scores, while the median 7 tells the truer story of how the class did.

blocks.x: Pick the statistic that answers the question. The same data set has a mean, a median, and a mode, and they can disagree sharply, so whoever reports just one of them gets to choose the impression it gives. A mean dragged upward by a few huge values can make a typical outcome look far better than it is, and a mode parked on some unrepresentative clump can do the same. Before you trust a summary, ask what the number is actually for. When a handful of extremes pull the mean away from the crowd, the median usually tells the fairer story, since it sits where the middle person really lands. And treat the word "average" as a warning sign whenever nobody says which one it means, because that silence is often where the misleading happens.

Five families have a median income of 40 thousand dollars. The two lowest earners make 10 thousand and 25 thousand. What is the smallest the total income of all five families could be? Give the number (in thousands).
hints: The median only pins down the middle family, so the third income when the five are sorted is fixed at 40. The two lowest are already given as 10 and 25, both below it.
The two families above the median can earn anything at or above 40. To make the total as small as possible, push both of them down as low as they are allowed to go, which is exactly 40.
blocks.sol: The median fixes only the middle value, not the total. Sort the five incomes. The median of 40 means the third one is 40, and the two given low earners, 10 and 25, sit below it. The two remaining families are above the median, so each can be anything at or above 40. To shrink the total, we let both of them sink all the way to 40, the smallest they are allowed to be. That gives $$10 + 25 + 40 + 40 + 40 = \boxed{155}$$ so the smallest possible total is 155 thousand dollars. The lesson is that a median tells you where the middle sits but says almost nothing about the total. The families above it could be at 40 or at 4000, and the median never changes.

blocks.x: A company has 10 people. Their mean salary is 100 thousand dollars and their median salary is 40 thousand dollars. The nine non-executive workers all earn exactly the same amount, and the tenth person is the CEO. How much does the CEO earn? Give the number (in thousands).
hints: The mean is the total shared out evenly, so run it backward. If 10 people average 100, the whole payroll must add up to \(100 \times 10 = 1000\) thousand.

With 10 people the median sits between the 5th and 6th salaries. Both of those are ordinary workers, and every worker earns the same, so each worker's salary is just 40. That pins down nine of the ten numbers.
blocks.sol: Both summaries are clues, and together they let us rebuild a number nobody told us. Work each statistic backward. The mean unlocks the total. Ten people averaging 100 means the payroll adds up to $$100 \times 10 = 1000 \text{ thousand}.$$ The median unlocks the workers. With ten salaries the median is the average of the 5th and 6th, and both of those are ordinary workers. Since all nine workers earn the same, that shared salary is 40. Nine of them take $$9 \times 40 = 360 \text{ thousand},$$ which leaves the CEO with $$1000 - 360 = \boxed{640} \text{ thousand}.$$ The CEO earns 640 thousand. Notice what tipped us off. The mean was 100 but the median was only 40, and that huge gap between them is the fingerprint of a single enormous outlier pulling the average up while the middle stays put. We did not just report the gap, we used it to reconstruct the exact number the two summaries were hiding.

blocks.label: Fun fact
blocks.x: In 1954 the journalist Darrell Huff published a slim book called "How to Lie with Statistics", showing how averages, graphs, and percentages get quietly bent to mislead. It went on to become one of the best selling statistics books ever written. That is a strange kind of fame for a math book, and it proves something worth remembering. Knowing what a number hides turns out to be just as valuable as knowing how to compute it.

Always ask what the number hides. A statistic is a summary, and every summary leaves something out. The mean is easily swayed by a single outlier and blind to how spread out the data is. The median ignores the extremes and tells you nothing about the total. The mode can sit on a lump of values that are not typical at all. And none of the three can be run backward into the data they came from. So keep one habit. Set the mean beside the median to sense skew and outliers, take a look at how far the values scatter, ask which summary the question actually needs, and never forget that one number cannot carry the whole story.

review.x: Two chess clubs each average 50 points per match. Club A scored 50, 50, and 50. Club B scored 10, 50, and 90. Find the range of Club B's scores. Give the number.
hints: Both clubs share the same mean, so the average alone treats them as identical. The range measures how far the scores spread out, which is exactly what the mean hides here. Subtract Club B's lowest score from its highest.
review.sol:
The mean hides spread. Club A and Club B both average 50, so on paper they look the same. But Club A never strayed from 50, while Club B swung from a low of 10 to a high of 90. The range captures that swing, giving

$$90 - 10 = \boxed{80}$$

So Club B's range is 80. The lesson is that two data sets can share the exact same mean yet behave completely differently. The average told us nothing about how consistent or wild the scores were, and the spread is what it kept hidden.

review.x: Ben played five basketball games and scored 88, 90, 85, 92, and 0 points. He fouled out early in one game, which is why one of his scores is 0. Find the median of his five scores. Give the number.
hints: The median is the middle value once the scores are in order, so line up all five from smallest to largest before you point to the middle one. Notice how the lonely 0 sits far to the left but does not touch the number in the center.
review.sol:
The median is the middle value of the ordered list, not the average, so start by sorting the five scores from smallest to largest.

$$0,\ 85,\ \boxed{88},\ 90,\ 92$$

With five values, the middle one is the third, which is \(88\). So Ben's median is \(88\) points.

Here is what this hides. That 0 came from fouling out and is a true outlier, and it drags the mean down to \(\frac{0+85+88+90+92}{5}=71\) points. The median barely notices it and stays at \(88\), right where his typical game lives. The mean says his scoring collapsed, the median says one game was a fluke and the rest were strong. The single number you pick decides the whole story.

review.x: Five houses on a street sold for 20, 22, 24, 26, and 58 ten-thousands of dollars. Find the mean and the median of these five prices, then give the mean minus the median. Give the number.
hints: The four cheaper houses cluster together and one is far above the rest. The mean gets pulled up by that one high price, while the median just sits at the middle house, so watch how far apart the two summaries end up.
review.sol:
The mean is sensitive to outliers, the median is not. Add all five prices and divide by five, giving
$$\frac{20+22+24+26+58}{5}=\frac{150}{5}=30.$$
The median is the middle value once they are in order, which is the third one, so the median is \(24\). The gap between them is
$$30-24=\boxed{6}.$$
Four of the five houses sold near 20 to 26 ten-thousands, yet the mean says 30. That single expensive house dragged the mean six ten-thousands above the median. The mean minus median is \(6\), and the lesson is that when the two summaries disagree this much, one high value is quietly hiding inside the average.

review.x: A team of 9 players has a median height of 70 inches. Someone reads that and pictures nine tall people. But the median only pins down the middle player once the heights are lined up in order. Four players are shorter than the middle one and four are taller, and nothing says how tall those four at the top can climb. What is the smallest possible height, in inches, of the tallest player on the team? Give the number in inches.
hints: Line the 9 heights up in order. The median is the 5th one, so it equals 70. The tallest player is the 9th, sitting above the median. Ask yourself how small that top value is allowed to be while still landing at or above 70.
review.sol: The median tells you about the middle, not the extremes. Put the 9 heights in order from shortest to tallest. With 9 players the median is the 5th value, so the 5th player is exactly 70 inches. Every player after the 5th is at least as tall as the median, so the four tallest players are each at least 70. To make the tallest player as short as possible, let all of them tie the median, giving a lineup where the top values are $$70,\ 70,\ 70,\ 70.$$ The tallest can be no shorter than that, so the answer is \(\boxed{70}\) inches. This is what the median hides. It fixes the middle but says nothing about how the tails behave, so a team with a median of 70 could be all the same height or could stretch far above it, and one summary number cannot tell you which.

review.x: A server's five weekly tips, in dollars, are 4, 8, 10, 14, and 20, so the median is 10. One night a grateful customer leaves a huge 9000 dollar tip, added as a sixth value. Find the median of all six tips. Give the number.
hints: The median only cares about the middle of the sorted list, not how large the biggest value is. With six numbers there is no single middle value, so the median is the average of the two in the center. Sort the six tips and look at which values land in those center spots.
review.sol: The idea here is that the median barely notices the giant tip. Sort the six values in order, giving 4, 8, 10, 14, 20, 9000. With an even count of numbers, the median is the average of the two middle ones, which are 10 and 14, so $$\frac{10 + 14}{2} = \boxed{12}.$$ Adding a value 9000 dollars larger than everything else moved the median only from 10 to 12. That is what the median hides and protects against. A single wild value can swing the mean enormously, but the median just shifts to the next spot in the sorted list. The new median is 12.

review.x: Five numbers have a mean of 12. Consider these four statements about them. (1) The five numbers add to 60. (2) At least one of them is 12 or more. (3) They are not all equal. (4) At least one of them is 12 or less. Some of these are forced by the mean and some are not. How many of the four statements must be true?
hints: The mean fixes the total, and it fixes where the balance point sits, but it says nothing about how spread out the values are. Test each statement by asking whether you can build five numbers that break it while keeping the mean at 12.
review.sol:
A mean pins down the total and the balance point, and nothing else. Since the mean is 12, the five numbers must add to
$$5 \times 12 = 60,$$
so statement (1) is forced. For (2) and (4), if every value were below 12 the total would fall short of 60, and if every value were above 12 the total would overshoot, so there has to be at least one value that is 12 or more and at least one that is 12 or less. Both are forced. Statement (3) is the trap. Five 12's average to 12 and are all equal, so the data does not have to be spread out at all. That leaves (1), (2), and (4) true and (3) not forced, giving $$\boxed{3}$$ statements that must be true. The lesson is that the mean tells you the total and the center, but it hides the spread. Equal data and wildly scattered data can share the exact same mean.

review.x: Team A has 20 players who average 90 points each. Team B has 20 players who average 30 points each. The two teams merge into one group of 40 players. Find the average of the merged group. Give the number.
hints: Because the two teams are the same size, no side pulls the average harder than the other. The merged average sits exactly halfway between 90 and 30, so you can just average the two averages.
review.sol: When two groups are the same size, you can average their averages, because each side carries equal weight. Halfway between 90 and 30 is $$\frac{90+30}{2}=\boxed{60}.$$ The merged group averages 60. Notice what the single number hides. A player who scores exactly 60 does not exist on either team. The average lands between the two clusters, in an empty gap where nobody actually is.

review.x: A gym advertises that its members' average number of monthly visits is 70. But a few members come almost every day, and their high counts pull that average up. The median number of visits is 40, which sits much closer to what most members actually do. Which number honestly describes a typical member, the average of 70 or the median of 40? Give the number.
hints: The average is 70 only because a handful of daily regulars sit far above everyone else, and one high value can drag the mean upward without changing what the crowd in the middle looks like. Ask which number stays near the typical member even when a few extremes are present.
review.sol:
The mean gets pulled toward extreme values, while the median reports the middle no matter how large the extremes are. A few daily regulars log huge visit counts, and those big numbers lift the mean to 70 even though most members are nowhere near that. The median ignores how far out the extremes stretch and simply marks the middle of the group, giving

$$\text{median} = \boxed{40}$$

So 40 is the honest description of a typical member. The lesson is that a single average can hide a lopsided group. When a few values sit far above the rest, the mean drifts toward them and stops describing anyone real, so the median is the number that tells the truth here.

review.x: A tutoring center has two study groups. Group A has 4 students who averaged 100 on a test, and Group B has 6 students who averaged 55 on the same test. All 10 students are combined into one list. Find the average score of the combined group. Give the number.
hints: You cannot just average 100 and 55, because the two groups are not the same size. The average is the total of every score divided by how many scores there are, so rebuild the total from each group's average times its number of students.
review.sol:
An average times the count gives back the total, so recover each group's point total and add them. Group A holds \(4 \times 100 = 400\) points and Group B holds \(6 \times 55 = 330\) points. Divide the combined total by all 10 students, giving

$$\frac{400 + 330}{10} = \frac{730}{10} = \boxed{73}.$$
\nThe combined average is \(73\). Notice it lands closer to \(55\) than to \(100\), because the larger group pulls the average toward its own value. That is the whole point of a weighted average, and it is exactly what a plain midpoint of \(77.5\) between \(55\) and \(100\) would hide.

review.x: Five stores report their daily sales in hundreds of dollars. The median of the five values is 30, and the two lowest stores sold 5 and 12. What is the smallest possible total for all five stores? Give the number.
hints: The median only fixes the middle value at 30, and it tells you nothing about how big the top two stores are. To make the total as small as it can be, ask how low the two stores above the median are allowed to go without breaking the rule that 30 sits in the middle.
review.sol: The trap here is thinking the median controls the total. It does not. The median just pins the middle value, leaving the top two stores free to be anything from 30 upward. To shrink the total as much as possible, push those top two stores all the way down to 30, the lowest they can be while 30 still sits in the middle. That gives the values 5, 12, 30, 30, 30, so the smallest total is $$5 + 12 + 30 + 30 + 30 = \boxed{107}.$$ The smallest possible total is 107 (hundreds of dollars). Notice what the median hid. A median of 30 puts no ceiling on the total at all. The real total could be far larger, since the top two stores might have sold thousands. One middle number tells you where the center sits, never how much sits above it.

review.x: Nine people rated a movie on a scale from 1 to 13. Their ratings were 2, 2, 2, 2, 9, 10, 11, 12, and 13. Someone reports the mode and says the typical rating was low. Find the median instead. Give the number.
hints: The mode is just the value that shows up most often, and here four low ratings of 2 make it the mode even though five of the nine people rated the movie high. The median asks a different question, so line the ratings up in order and find the one that sits right in the middle.
review.sol:
The mode picks whatever value appears most, and four ratings of 2 give it the win. But that ignores that most people rated the movie high, so the mode paints the wrong picture here. The median fixes this by finding the true middle. The nine ratings are already in order, and with nine values the middle one is the 5th, giving

$$2,\ 2,\ 2,\ 2,\ \boxed{9},\ 10,\ 11,\ 12,\ 13$$

The median is 9. That single number sits far above the mode of 2, and it tells the honest story. The mode hid the fact that most people liked the movie, while the median showed the middle of the crowd.

review.x: Ten test scores have a mean of 60. Nine of the ten scores are each 50. Find the tenth score. Give the number.
hints: The mean pins down the total, not the individual pieces. Ten scores averaging 60 must add to a fixed sum, so work out that sum first and then see what is left over once you remove the nine known scores.
review.sol:
A mean is a promise about the total, not about any one number. If ten scores average 60, their sum is locked at
$$10 \times 60 = 600.$$
Nine of them are each 50, contributing \(9 \times 50 = 450\). The tenth score has to make up the difference, giving
$$600 - 450 = \boxed{150}.$$
So the tenth score is 150. Notice what the summary hid. A mean of 60 sounds like a room full of ordinary scores near 60, but here nine scores sit far below it and one score towers far above. The average alone never warns you that one value is doing all the heavy lifting.
